title: Archangel Gabriel (Monthly) — St. Jude the Apostle (Thaddeus) & St. Zosimus the Martyr
narrative:
title": "Archangel Gabriel (Monthly) — St. Jude the Apostle (Thaddeus) & St. Zosimus the Martyr

narrative": "The Monthly Commemoration of the Holy Archangel Gabriel the Annunciator, St. Jude the Apostle also called Thaddeus the Brother of the Lord, and St. Zosimus the Soldier-Martyr

On the nineteenth of Sene, the holy Church celebrates again the monthly commemoration of the holy Archangel Gabriel, whose feast returns for the second time in this month as the month moves toward the great apostolic culmination of its final days. The second appearance of Gabriel in Sene is the tradition's way of framing the approach of the feast of Peter and Paul: as the month began with Gabriel's annunciation presence and moves now through its latter half toward the princes of the apostles, the angel of the Incarnation returns to remind the community that the apostolic mission whose triumphant conclusion the feast of Peter and Paul celebrates was set in motion by the very word that Gabriel carried to the Virgin in Nazareth. The apostles exist because the Word became flesh, and the Word became flesh because Gabriel said 'Hail, full of grace' and the Virgin said 'Let it be done to me according to your word.' The angel who opened the Gospel is present again as the Gospel moves toward one of its greatest celebrations.

The theology of Gabriel in his second Sene appearance is therefore a theology of the connecting thread: the annunciation and the apostolate are not two separate events in sacred history but two moments in a single continuous action of God — the divine word going out from the Father, taking flesh in the Virgin, teaching and healing and dying and rising in the body assumed from her, and then sending out through the apostles the same word that Gabriel first carried, now clothed in the flesh and blood of the Resurrection. Gabriel is the tradition's icon of this continuity: the angel who carried the word to Mary is the same angel who, in the tradition, assists and protects the apostolic mission, and his monthly return in the calendar is the community's reminder that the angelic ministry of annunciation is not finished, that every proclamation of the Gospel by every preacher in every generation is a new carrying of the same word that Gabriel first brought to human ears.

Saint Jude the Apostle, called Thaddeus and identified in some traditions as the brother of the Lord, enters this feast as one of the more mysteriously compelling of the Twelve: the apostle who is distinguished from Judas Iscariot by the very name Thaddeus, as if the tradition were anxious to protect him from any confusion with the betrayer, and who in John's Gospel asks the single question that stands as one of the most theologically searching in all of apostolic literature: 'Lord, how is it that you will manifest yourself to us, and not to the world?' The question is the question of every subsequent generation of the Church — the question of why the risen Christ appeared to the disciples and not to the whole world simultaneously, why the Gospel must be carried by witnesses rather than proclaimed by the risen Lord himself in visible glory — and Jesus's answer, which speaks of love and keeping the word and the indwelling of the Father and the Son, is the tradition's foundational theology of the Church as the place of the divine presence in the world.

Saint Zosimus the Soldier-Martyr joins Jude on this feast as a military witness whose conversion and martyrdom follow a pattern familiar in the tradition but no less moving for its familiarity: a soldier who encountered the Christian faith, likely through the witness of other Christians he met in the course of his military service, and who was so seized by what he encountered that he was unable to continue his service when it required him to participate in persecution. Zosimus's story is the tradition's testimony that the Roman army, which was one of the primary instruments of Christian persecution, was also — through the encounters it created between soldiers and martyrs — one of the primary channels of Christian conversion: the men sent to guard the martyrs, to execute them, to clean up after their deaths, saw things in the dying of the faithful that changed them irrevocably, and the Church that emerged from the period of persecution carried in its martyrology the names of dozens of soldiers who had been converted by the very deaths they were supposed to be administering. The nineteenth day of Sene gathers the angel of the word, the apostle who asked the right question, and the soldier converted by the answer that other martyrs' deaths provided. Amen.
